steps.title: Square to make a quadratic
steps.content: The equation is $$\sqrt{k-x}=29-x.$$ Since the left side is nonnegative, any real solution must have $29-x\ge 0$, or $x\le 29$. Squaring gives $$k-x=(29-x)^2.$$

steps.title: Put the quadratic in standard form
steps.content: Expand and move everything to one side: $$k-x=x^2-58x+841$$ $$0=x^2-57x+841-k.$$

steps.title: Force exactly one real solution
steps.content: For the quadratic to have exactly one real solution, set the discriminant equal to $0$: $$57^2-4(1)(841-k)=0$$ $$3249-3364+4k=0$$ $$4k=115.$$

steps.title: Check the domain condition
steps.content: The repeated root is $x=\frac{57}{2}=28.5$, and $28.5\le 29$, so it satisfies the original radical equation's requirement. Therefore the minimum possible value of $4k$ is 115.
